figures.caption: FIGURE 7: AI judge score trends for the full pipeline versus the ablation baseline across revision rounds in the phone stand case study (Set A) and Cantilever Beam Case Study (Set B). Linear regression slopes confirm that the full pipeline maintains a positive or near-zero trend, while the ablation shows stagnation or slight degradation.

FIGURE 13: Successful Replicates. The set of replicates in which at least one design in the sequence aligns with the human designer’s request for a branching, tree-like skeletal structure. Replicates A through F correspond to separate runs of the same pipeline in the phone-stand case study, where each sequence of revisions reflects the outcomes produced by passing through the agentic pipeline. The score from the AI judge is presented below each design where scores are made relative to the other revisions within the same replicate.

FIGURE 1: The TO-Agents pipeline consisting of a sequence of subagents that interact with a human designer in a multi-turn conversation architecture. Agents iteratively adjust the design parameters until the resulting structure satisfies the human designer’s qualitative objective of a bioinspired, dendriform architecture with hierarchical branching. Injection of judge agent feedback enables the system toward this design goal as the number of revisions grows.

FIGURE 4: Two case studies illustrating failed multi-turn revision trajectories across the full TOAgents pipeline: phone stand case study (top) and cantilever beam case study (bottom). Both case studies are initialized with the same human preference to increase the hierarchical dendriform visual complexity of the overall topology. AI Judge scores for each successive revision are noted below each design.
TABLE 1: Parameter trajectory across revisions for Figure 13 replicate B.

FIGURE 14: Failure Replicates. The set of replicates in which none of the designs in the sequence align with the human designer’s request for a branching, tree-like skeletal structure. Replicates A through D correspond to separate failure cases in the phone-stand case study, where each sequence of revisions reflects the outcomes produced by passing through the agentic pipeline. The score from the AI judge is presented below each design where scores are made relative to the other revisions within the same replicate.

FIGURE 6: (A) Mean scores across revisions in Set A (the phone stand case study), showing similar upward trends for both human raters and the AI judge. (B) Confusion matrix for Set A ratings, showing human–AI agreement (32% exact agreement, 72% within ±1, κ = 0.138). (B right) Pairwise agreement comparisons for the Set A task, where human–AI agreement is positive but lower than human–human agreement. (C) Mean scores across revisions in Set B (the cantilever beam study), where human scores improve while AI scores remain more stable but follow the overall trend. (D) Confusion matrix for Set B ratings, showing weaker human–AI alignment (20% exact agreement, 68% within ±1, κ = −0.062). (D right) Pairwise agreement comparisons for the Set B task, where human–AI agreement falls near the lower end of the human inter-rater range.

FIGURE 2: Manufacturing agent prepares the best design made by the agentic system for additive manufacturing which includes post-processing the raw TO structure with solid padding followed by meshing of dense regions. This structure can be directly imported and sliced in a 3D-printing slicer. We verify this pipeline end-to-end with the final print shown in the result.
FIGURE 8: System prompt supplied to the AI judge agent, organized into Identity, Context, Priority, and Task layers.

FIGURE 15: Successful Replicates. In the cantilever beam case study, we focus on the set of replicates in which at least one design in the sequence aligns with the human designer’s request for a branching, tree-like skeletal structure. Replicates A through F correspond to separate runs of the process, where each sequence of revisions reflects the outcomes produced by passing through the agentic pipeline. The score from the AI judge is presented below each design where scores are made relative to the other revisions within the same replicate.

FIGURE 9: System prompt supplied to the vision agent, organized into Identity, Context, Priority, and Task layers. The Priority block is templated with the best-scoring revision and its judge score, which are filled in at each turn.
FIGURE 10: Revision instructions injected into the vision agent’s context at each turn, governing how it ingests prior iterations and what hard constraints it must respect.

FIGURE 16: Failure Replicates. In the cantilever beam case study, we focus on the set of replicates in which none of the designs in the sequence align with the human designer’s request for a branching, tree-like skeletal structure. Replicates A through D correspond to separate failure cases of the process, where each sequence of revisions reflects the outcomes produced by passing through the agentic pipeline. The score from the AI judge is presented below each design where scores are made relative to the other revisions within the same replicate.
TABLE 3: Observed recovery in parameter values across revisions for Figure 13 replicate C.
FIGURE 12: Cantilever-beam case study: the human designer specifies the FEA setup, boundary conditions (red), applied loads (green), and TO parameters as a single natural-language request.
FIGURE 11: Phone-stand case study: the human designer specifies the FEA setup, boundary conditions (red), applied loads (green), and TO parameters as a single natural-language request.

FIGURE 3: Two case studies illustrating successful multi-turn revision trajectories across the full TOAgents pipeline: phone stand case study (top) and cantilever beam case study (bottom). Both case studies are initialized with the same human preference to increase the hierarchical dendriform visual complexity of the overall topology. AI Judge scores for each successive revision are noted below each design.
